sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
I must have asked around and found some kind of desk, maybe the finance office, pay master.
Nobody seemed to be expecting me to arrive.
I mean, I can't imagine anybody was holding their breath.

What happened then was the first thing I did was I decided I needed some German money, and I may have been the only person who was ever associated with the American occupation who legally exchanged money.
The legal rate was three Reichsmarks to the dollar.
The black market rate was 200 Reichsmarks to the dollar.
Nobody ever made the mistake I made.
I think I changed $10, which gave me nothing.
Reichsmarks were worthless.
I quickly got the atmosphere of Nuremberg, which was that all transactions at a consumer level were done for cigarettes.
Cigarettes were the key to life in Germany.
You-- you tip people, you hired people for cigarettes.
Within a couple weeks, I hired a young German as a chauffeur when I got a car, not within a couple weeks, it was really several months, but for about two or three packs of cigarettes a week.
Cigarettes cost in the PX $0.05 a pack.
So two or three packs of cigarettes a week would cost me what?
A dollar and a half or something.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
But before I tell you about my official assignments, the atmosphere of Nuremberg was something.
I'd been in the war but mostly almost uninhabited places, I mean Saipan had a civilian population, but it was all farm population, plantation population.
It had no real urban centers.
It had a couple of small towns.
Nuremberg was a shambles, so was Frankfurt.
They both had the basic characteristics.
No-- no train station had a roof.
It had all been bombed off.
The bombing was almost random, except the center of Nuremberg, which was the old town and was a walled city within the city was a total wreck.
The wall was demolished.
Almost everything in the middle of town was destroyed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans walked around like quasi zombies, you know, carrying briefcases with lunches or whatever food they could get.
You could see furniture of the civilian population being moved out to the farms in exchange for food.
German farmers were plundering the cities.
All of this gradually grew on me.
The only transportation around town were trolleys.
Very few cars and whatever they were were American army cars.
You'd occasionally see a German civilian car with some kind of cart to burn coal.
I don't know how the hell they generated any usable fuel.
But they seemed to be operating on a kind of a coal energy system.
But I quickly adapted.
I-- I had-- was building out in a suburb in a rather nice house that had been owned by some Nazi party figure and been requisitioned by the occupation authorities.
Thing I liked about it is it had a shower with six different spouts that hit you from different directions.
Huh?
No, no, no, no.
The first place I lived in had about, I would say three or four members of the prosecution staff besides me.
It was a fair-size house.
We all had different rooms.
I had quite a good ground-floor room.
I remember one of the people who lived there was a fellow, an Austrian Jew named [?
Scalla ?] who was always run down, as well he might have been.
His-- his family had been exterminated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was another fellow who was an interpreter who had a German title of nobility who was half Jewish named von [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were a couple more there.
But I-- at the court house, I'm not even sure how I got my orders.

It may have been that I went in to see Telford Taylor who was the chief prosecutor at this point, but I was assigned immediately to read all the transcripts of all the cases that had been decided to date and prepare a manual for the use of the prosecution on evidentiary rulings.
And that was quite educational, got me into the transcript of the main Nuremberg trial and also the ongoing proceedings which were under way then, the justice case, the doctor's case.
There were a great variety of cases.
And it also got me into documentation for these cases, which I found horrifying.
I mean, these were German accounts of German conduct and they were totally horrifying.
And I would read this stuff and get drenched in it all day long, meanwhile plucking out a few evidentiary rulings as I went along, but I got absorbed in the substance as well.
And the documentation, as far as I was concerned, was more horrible than any of the fiction I've ever read or seen.
And this went on for months.
I just kept on reading the stuff and reading the stuff.

So during the day, generally, my reaction was one of enormous disgust and contempt and a lot of adrenaline shooting into my blood streams, and then I would walk out on the street in the middle of this German population, not knowing who I was ever dealing with, you know?
Absorbing all the laws of civilized discourse and conduct, politeness, and you didn't know whether the next guy you were talking to was an ex-SS officer or what.
So I was always guarded in my dealings with Germans.
We were also under a general non-fraternization rule, which I violated from almost the first day I got there.
So I spent a lot of time in German bars and German cafes and a fair amount of time with German girls.
Why did you do that?
Because it was entertaining, because I wanted to know what I was into.
I wanted to know what the population was like, what were the basic views?
And I discovered the reason Nazi Germany was Nazi was because a lot of Germans were Nazis.
And what was their basic attitude?
The Nazis were fine except they brought misery on us.
They lost, that was their vice.
I made it-- made it clear fairly quickly.
No, I wasn't known because if you can talk of Jewish characteristics, I didn't have any of those.
I wasn't-- and the name certainly was no giveaway.
It sounded more German than it sounded Jewish, although it happens to be the authentic original name.
No.
No, the Germans had gotten over a fair amount of that by the time.
Of course, it took a while before they revived it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No.
I made it a point always to make it evident that I was Jewish, otherwise you'd get into some very uncomfortable situations.
No, no.
Although, I probably did more in Germany than I ever have any place else because it was a matter of showing the flag.
I mean, in other words, I went to services for Passover, I went to services for the high holidays.
Normally I wouldn't do either because I'm not really religious.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm Jewish for a different reason, both my parents were.
Well, PX was the post exchange, which the Army maintained at the courthouse.
US Army.
That's where I ran into my wife.
Second wife, yes.
Her name?
She was Estonian.
She still is Estonian.
Her name was Aino [?
Allekand ?] [?
Uis. ?] Aino, being a typical Estonian or Finnish first name.
No.
She was strictly off a farm in Estonia.
She was young.
What was she doing?
She had-- when the war broke out, I guess she was about 15 years old.
She lived first under Russian occupation, then German occupation.
When the Russians came back again she took off for Sweden in a fishing boat.
The Germans picked her up in the Baltic Sea and shipped her off to a labor camp near Dresden where she thinks she was working on B2 missiles.
The labor camp consisted of the Russians, Pols, Belgians, anything the Germans could dragoon up.
I think she lived on turnips and cabbage for about a year, and that was it.
Then when the Russians came into East Germany, which was where she was, she walked about 200 miles to the American zone.
She knew English.
She had studied English.
She knew German, she knew some Russian.
She had Russian relatives.
She went to work, I think, American Red Cross for a while.
Then she went to work for the PX.
Oh yes, it was deep in the American zone.
At this time, Germany had four zones, the Western zones had not been consolidated.
Of course, it was while I was there, a little bit later in 1948, that the Berlin airlifts stopped-- started.
But she was a cashier at the PX, she also had control of the cigarette counter.
And I-- she offered me-- this was before I really knew her very well, but she offered me un-rationed cigarettes.
We were rationed to a carton a week, that controlled the black market to a fair degree, but she had control of this and she could offer me extra cigarettes if I wanted them.
But I was so dumb and straight laced that I never took any.
I refused.
She regards that as very peculiar my part.
Oh yes.
Says she didn't make that offer to anybody except me, and she can't imagine anybody who would have turned it down.
But I didn't think in terms of goods or money in those days.
I was totally unrealistic.
No, no, Nazi party hierarchy, that's different.
There were very few Nazi party officials who were German nobility.
Well, it was a two-- yeah, they were all-- all of them were colleagues from the Nuremberg proceedings.
There were translators and interpreters.
There were also a couple, now that I start thinking back on it, a couple of Swiss who were there as interpreters.
Don't let me forget to mention my episodes with the great German airplane designer Willy Messerschmitt, because that was an interesting episode.
The Swiss were courting him.
Willy Messerschmitt was held in Nuremberg as a material witness.
He was not a defendant, but had been held for a considerable period of time.
I mean, he had freedom to wander in the city but everybody was courting Willy Messerschmitt.
Messerschmitt airplanes, yeah.
Yeah, he was a designer.
Because he was a famous airplane designer.
Because Messerschmitts were pretty good planes.
He did the design for the airplanes, I don't think he did anything on the engines.
But the Swiss were courting him, the British were courting him, I don't think the French were, but the Americans certainly were.
The Americans were ready to pick up anybody in Germany that would be useful.
Bear in mind that by '47, '48, the Cold War was underway and the thinking was not in terms of World War II.
It was in terms of the next one.
As a result of which, the United States, among other countries, brought in a great number of German scientists, about half of whom had a very thorough Nazi taint.
As you well know.
The Swiss brought him to our billet.
We had a cook.
We could make our meals there.
We had a German cook.
They brought him as a dinner guest one evening, and I was sitting in.
I was probably, oh, I wasn't so damn old.
I was probably the senior member of the group in the billet.

So I-- I sat to dinner with him and I quickly concluded he was a first class son of a bitch who explained to everybody assembled that of course the Germans had only killed about 100,000 Jews, and they were all criminals.

And the problem and the reason why the Germans had to launch the war was because they had restricted markets, and couldn't trade much, and the rest of the world had been oppressing Germany and threw out the usual Nazi garbage to the effect that all of this was a consequence of very bad treatment in the Versailles Treaty and post-World War I.
So I knew who I was dealing with.
Did you see fit to contradict him in that regard?
Yeah, of course I contradicted him, but I didn't carry on a prolonged argument with him.
I just told the Swiss that was the last time he was coming to that house.
And that was the case, they didn't bring him again.
No, not to begin with.
As I say, I was assigned to-- it was a make work job for about a month to read the transcripts and pick out evidentiary rulings and to prepare a manual on procedure and evidence.
Which I did.
Yeah, it must have been 100, 150 pages of various rulings here, there, and everywhere, disputes about translation.
The prosecution lawyers.
Huh?
Oh yeah, he was effectively Jackson's successor as the head of the American group, and very able, very able.
What?
Jackson was able too, but I'm talking about Taylor.
I was assigned.
I was assigned to a tentative, projected case against German banks, plural, particularly the Dresdner and Deutsche Banks.
Deutsche was the largest bank in Germany, Dresdner was the second largest bank.

And we were working up a case against a fellow named Hermann Abs, who was a very famous German banker, good connections with Lehman Brothers, and a case against a couple of board members of the Dresdner Bank who were also SS officers and Friends of Himmler.
When I say friends of Himmler, there was a circle called the Friends of Himmler and they were members of the circle.
The Dresdner Bank and the Deutsche Bank completed in taking over Jewish properties, either before or after their owners were killed.
In other words, they were ghouls.
I can get more into that, but anyhow, I worked together with a couple of other lawyers.
There was an economist in the US Treasury who had been active in pursuing the record of these banks during the war, fellow named [?
Forster ?] Adams who became convinced he didn't like these banks and was kind of zealous about it.
And he had whipped up a fair amount of material.
I was the bottom man in a trial team that was projected to three people.
The other two were Rawlings [?
Ragland, ?] who was a damn good lawyer from Washington and whose going to Nuremberg cost him literally millions of dollars.
He was one of the original founders of Geico Insurance Company and he sold out all of his stock at the beginning of Geico when he went to Nuremberg.
And the other fellow was a graduate of Columbia Law School who had a couple years of practice named Herb Goldenberg.
And I roomed with Goldenberg in an office.
We worked together.
Goldenberg, of course, was a much more knowledgeable and better lawyer than I was, but a very quiet fellow.
And we were on very good terms.
And I liked Rawlings Ragland too, he was a very able lawyer, experienced too.
He had worked with-- primarily with-- what the hell's his name?
I can't remember it, a fellow who did legal work in Puerto Rico, quite a well-known American lawyer.
Tugwell.
Rawlings had been lawyer assistant to Rex Tugwell, who was known in the New Deal days of the '30s.
Anyhow, that was the team and we-- we started to investigate heavily.
We collected literally thousands of pages of Dresdner Bank records, Deutsche Bank records.
We stumbled upon a report prepared in the Dresdner Bank on so-called aryanization.
This was about a 200-page report on the marvels that the Dresdner Bank had accomplished in taking over Jewish properties, to the extent of two, three billion Reichsmarks.
Primarily within Germany, but in Czechoslovakia heavily and elsewhere.
They were very proud of this and somebody prepared a book on it.
All in one place.
I didn't find it.
It was delivered to me by someone who did find it.
We had investigators and interpreters.
I had a-- my primary reliance was a guy named Otto [?
Verber ?] who was an Austrian Jew who had left Vienna somewhere around 1938 and served with distinction in the American army as an intelligence officer.
And he was damn good.
He knew Germany.
He knew Austria.
He knew the politics and he-- obviously he was very good in Germany.
He was a-- German was his native language.
He was like a chemistry undergraduate, I think, in one of the German or Austrian universities from Vienna before he left the country, when the Germans came in.
Must have been '38.
And he turned up a lot of this stuff and did a lot of the translation.
He was fairly acute.
I'll get further into his background later.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyhow, somewhere towards the end of '47 or maybe the beginning of '48, Taylor called all of us into his office and said that the trials were going to be curtailed.
We were out of step with the whole occupation.
We were busy prosecuting Germans at a time when official policy was to secure the Germans for our side in the Cold War.
The Russians were, of course, doing the same thing in their areas.
And therefore, we were really out of step.
I mean, nothing we were doing was gonna make the Germans fonder of the United States.
We were kind of a vestige of the war and out of date.
So the bank cases were proposed to be reduced from two banks to one, with one defendant from the bank.
So Taylor first said, is there anything to these bank cases?
Are they worth a damn?
And Rawlings and Goldenberg said, yup, there was some merit to them, but they were tough cases and might not be able to be proven.
By this time, the atmosphere in the courts was not all that favorable either.
We had a bunch of state court judges, some of whom were-- not many, but at least a couple of them were damn near pro-German.
These were US judges?
Oh yeah, these were all state court judges, generally supreme courts of the various states.
They were invited by the Department of Justice of the War Department to go in Nuremberg and some of them decided to do it.
So these, Goldenberg and [?
Ragland ?] had some reservations about whether the cases would be successful, but I didn't have any reservations.
I didn't know any better.
I said, yeah, I think the case is good.
Taylor said, you want to work on it?
I said, yeah, I want to keep working on it.
So Rawlings, [?
Ragland, ?] and Herb Goldenberg were switched to the Krupp Trial team, and they really ran that team in good part successfully.
But I then became the sole prosecutor in the bank case, which was Karl Rasche.
Among his other functions, he was an SS colonel.
And that was pretty good.
And the Dresden Bank had an old saying which translated, 'Who goes before the first German tank?
It is Dr.
Karl Rasche of the Dresdner Bank.' That sounded good to me too.
I liked that.
It had the right flavor.
Then we had this huge aryanization report.
The Deutsche Bank, under Abs, had done exactly the same things.
Abs?
No question, Abs, and we would have indicted Abs.
Instead, Abs went on to become either a cabinet member under Adenauer, or very close to it.
He was a very prominent German banker.
The ultimate German banker in the post-war period, with, as I say, great connections to Lehman Brothers.
He put out a book on him.
Oh, because we were down to one man, and an SS colonel was better than a Nazi party member, and we had this aryanization report.
I think I was part of the selection as to which way we went and Rasche was my choice.
So I got the Rasche case exclusively and became a trial team all by myself, right out of the law school.
No.
I was it.
Now, I was part of the ministry's case, which was a big catch-all case.
It was really a last case.
It was case number 11 of 12, but 12, I think, was almost over by the time this one was formed.
Yes, yes.
Then after awhile, they added another defendant for me in the form of Emil Puhl who was a vice president of the Reichsbank.
The president had been acquitted in the big trial.
I was shocked, but I got the vice president.
Yes.
And then they added still another defendant who was Hans Kehrl, who was undersecretary of the Ministry of Economics.
These were all my defendants exclusively.
So I had a pretty good role.
And then I-- they threw a couple other pieces of cases at me, Plieger who was-- Hermann Goring Steelworks, which was a government-owned, biggest steel work in Germany.
And I had a small participation, very small, not even official, in the Flick case.
Flick, if you bought a Mercedes Benz in the period 1950, 1960, you were buying one of Flick's products.
Flick was a steel magnet and a Nazi before the war and was convicted separately in a different case.
There was a Flick case, but I had friends who were working on that and periodically I'd give them a hand on some aspect.
Adjourned.
Bathroom time.
